refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability of ≥0.97 (97%) over the customer-defined evaluation period, measured as the ratio of uptime to total operational time, accounting for planned maintenance, unplanned downtime, and safety-driven shutdowns.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-004 is a key performance indicator (KPI) that directly impacts mission success and stakeholder confidence. Refinement clarifies the measurement basis (uptime ratio), evaluation period scope, and inclusion of safety-driven events. High safety criticality requires explicit acknowledgment that availability must not compromise safety protocols.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Continuous telemetry logging of operational state transitions (active, idle, maintenance, fault, safety-hold); statistical analysis of uptime/downtime over agreed evaluation windows; correlation with maintenance records and safety event logs.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance and fault detection.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall enable rapid recovery from faults without compromising safety.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to Site Manager and Maintenance teams.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain safety-critical functions even during partial system degradation.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant subsystems (vehicle, teleoperation, safety services) with automatic failover and health monitoring to achieve target availability through architectural resilience.
DP-B: Deploy predictive maintenance algorithms and real-time fault detection across all modules, coupled with rapid parts inventory and on-site repair capability to minimize downtime.
DP-C: Establish a hierarchical availability management system: continuous telemetry collection → anomaly detection → predictive alerts → automated mitigation → human intervention protocols.
DP-D: Combine redundancy (DP-A) with predictive maintenance (DP-B) and availability management (DP-C) in an integrated framework that balances cost, complexity, and safety.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Integrated Availability Architecture combining (1) redundant critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) predictive maintenance and real-time fault detection, and (3) hierarchical availability management with telemetry-driven anomaly detection and automated mitigation protocols.

potential_sources: Redundancy design (DP-A) is independent of predictive maintenance algorithms (DP-B); both are decoupled at the architectural level.
Availability management system (DP-C) depends on telemetry interfaces from Vehicle and Safety Services modules; this is a known, manageable dependency.

Safety criticality constraint is satisfied: redundancy ensures safety functions remain active; predictive maintenance prevents safety-critical failures; availability management includes safety-hold states.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D exhibits acceptable coupling: functional domains (redundancy, maintenance, management) are orthogonal; physical/logical dependencies are well-defined and traceable to known interfaces (Telemetry, SafetyAlert).

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Redundant subsystems can be procured and integrated within cost and schedule constraints.
Assumption: Predictive maintenance algorithms can be trained on historical NGHE fleet data or equivalent equipment data.
Risk: Redundancy increases system complexity and potential failure modes in failover logic; mitigation: rigorous testing and formal verification of failover state machines.
Risk: Predictive maintenance accuracy depends on data quality and sensor calibration; mitigation: continuous sensor health monitoring and algorithm retraining.

Risk: Availability target (0.97) may be unachievable if root causes of downtime are external (e.g., site conditions, operator error); mitigation: clarify customer definition of 'operational availability' and exclude external factors.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly separate concerns: (1) Redundancy Strategy (subsystem-level failover), (2) Predictive Maintenance Strategy (algorithm-driven fault prediction), (3) Availability Monitoring Strategy (telemetry aggregation and anomaly detection). This separation will improve traceability to sub-FRs and enable independent IM assignment.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Top-level system integration module responsible for orchestrating availability across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services subsystems. Owns the availability management framework, redundancy coordination, and telemetry aggregation for KPI measurement.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational state, health metrics, and fault signals from Vehicle and Safety Services modules; used for availability calculation and anomaly detection.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Receives safety-critical alerts from Safety Services; sends availability-related safety holds and failover commands to ensure safety is never compromised by availability actions.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives mission tasking from Site Manager; availability status influences task scheduling and acceptance.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Provides communication backbone for redundancy coordination, telemetry transport, and failover signaling across distributed subsystems.

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the system-level integrator and owns the availability KPI. All sub-components (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) report to this module. Minimal diffusion: availability logic is centralized here, not scattered across subsystems.

Moderate containment: Vehicle owns its own health monitoring and fault detection, but availability orchestration (failover, redundancy coordination) must reside at system level. Diffusion risk: if availability logic is embedded in Vehicle, it becomes difficult to coordinate with Teleoperation and Safety Services.

Low containment: Teleoperation is a subsystem that contributes to availability but does not own the KPI. Availability management for Teleoperation (e.g., network redundancy, operator failover) should be coordinated at system level, not isolated within Teleoperation.

Moderate containment: Safety Services owns safety-critical availability (e.g., ensuring safety functions remain active during faults), but system-level availability orchestration must remain in NGHE Solution to avoid safety-availability conflicts.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the optimal owner of the availability KPI because: (1) it is the system-level integrator with visibility across all subsystems, (2) it owns the telemetry aggregation and anomaly detection logic, (3) it can coordinate redundancy and failover without creating diffusion across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services, (4) it can enforce safety-availability constraints at the system level.
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution may become a bottleneck if availability management logic is overly centralized; mitigation: use event-driven architecture and asynchronous messaging to distribute load.
Risk: Telemetry interface may become a single point of failure if not properly redundant; mitigation: implement redundant telemetry paths and ensure Network Link provides failover.

Risk: Coordination between availability management and safety services may introduce latency; mitigation: define clear priority rules (safety always overrides availability) and use real-time communication protocols.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Explicitly define the availability management subsystem within NGHE Solution as a separate logical component (e.g., 'Availability Manager') that owns telemetry aggregation, anomaly detection, and failover orchestration. This clarifies the boundary and enables independent testing and evolution.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (Integrated Availability Architecture) directly addresses FR-NGHE-004 by combining redundancy, predictive maintenance, and availability management. The DP is measurable (availability ratio), achievable (proven technologies), and safety-aware (includes safety-hold states). No functional gap identified.

diffusion_risks: Moderate risk: Predictive maintenance algorithms may be distributed across Vehicle and Safety Services modules; mitigation: centralize algorithm logic in NGHE Solution, with subsystem-specific data adapters in Vehicle and Safety Services.
Low risk: Redundancy failover logic is well-contained in NGHE Solution; Network Link provides the necessary communication backbone.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution provides strong containment for availability management. Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Tasking, and Network Link interfaces are well-defined and traceable. No significant diffusion expected if availability logic is not duplicated across subsystems.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High: Availability can be measured directly from telemetry logs (uptime/downtime ratio). Verification is feasible through continuous monitoring and statistical analysis over the agreed evaluation period.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear: NGHE Solution owns the availability KPI and has clear interfaces to Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services for data collection and failover coordination. Allocation of sub-FRs (predictive maintenance, fault detection, status visibility) to subsystems is straightforward.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D to separate concerns: (1) Redundancy Strategy, (2) Predictive Maintenance Strategy, (3) Availability Monitoring Strategy. This will improve traceability and enable independent IM assignment for each strategy.
Define explicit availability measurement protocol: specify telemetry data points, calculation method, evaluation window, and exclusion criteria (e.g., planned maintenance, external factors).
Establish safety-availability priority rules: document how safety-critical shutdowns are counted in availability calculations and how failover decisions respect safety constraints.

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-004 is a system-level KPI that requires decomposition into sub-FRs to enable detailed design and implementation. The refined FR and selected DP are sound, but the DP is too broad (combining three distinct strategies) to map directly to implementation modules without further refinement. Decomposition into sub-FRs will enable clearer DP selection and IM assignment for each strategy.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall minimize unplanned downtime through predictive maintenance and fault detection.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall enable rapid recovery from faults without compromising safety.
FR-NGHE-004.3: The NGHE solution shall provide real-time availability status visibility to Site Manager and Maintenance teams.
FR-NGHE-004.4: The NGHE solution shall maintain safety-critical functions even during partial system degradation.

assumptions: Operational availability is defined as uptime / (uptime + unplanned downtime), excluding planned maintenance and external factors.
Customer-defined evaluation period is at least 30 days of continuous operation to ensure statistical significance.
Redundant subsystems can be procured and integrated within cost and schedule constraints.
Predictive maintenance algorithms can be trained on historical NGHE fleet data or equivalent equipment data.
Safety-critical functions are always prioritized over availability optimization; safety-driven shutdowns do not count as downtime.
open_risks: Availability target (0.97) may be unachievable if root causes of downtime are external (e.g., site conditions, operator error); requires clarification with customer.
Redundancy increases system complexity and potential failure modes in failover logic; requires rigorous testing and formal verification.
Predictive maintenance accuracy depends on data quality and sensor calibration; requires continuous sensor health monitoring and algorithm retraining.
Telemetry interface may become a single point of failure if not properly redundant; requires redundant telemetry paths and failover mechanisms.
Coordination between availability management and safety services may introduce latency; requires clear priority rules and real-time communication protocols.
evidence_needed: Historical downtime data from similar NGHE systems or comparable heavy equipment to validate achievability of 0.97 target.
Predictive maintenance algorithm performance metrics (precision, recall, false positive rate) on representative fault scenarios.
Redundancy failover test results demonstrating automatic switchover without safety compromise.
Telemetry system reliability analysis (MTBF, redundancy coverage) to ensure availability measurement is not compromised.
Safety-availability interaction analysis documenting priority rules and edge cases (e.g., failover during safety-critical operation).
